cataloging_subject.label: Guy of Warwick (Later Couplet version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: One continuous 11,976-line Middle English romance redaction, including the Guy narrative through line 10,786 and its integral Harrawde/Reinbrun continuation at lines 10,787-11,976. The two movements are nested analytical parts, not separately counted works. Modern edition front matter, notes, glossary/index matter, page-image navigation, and physical manuscript carriers remain metadata or lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: Guy of Warwick (Later Couplet version)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator-adapter
catalog_summary.region: South Lincolnshire (probable linguistic origin of the redaction)

notes: Later Couplet is retained as a redaction label; the parenthetical qualifier distinguishes this work from other Middle English Guy of Warwick versions.

titles.value: The Romance of Guy of Warwick: The Second or 15th-Century Version
notes: This is the source-edition title, not an independent medieval title or a current composition-date claim.
Use only with the redaction qualifier where confusion with other Middle English versions is possible.

titles.value: Sith the time that God was born

agents.name: Julius Zupitza
notes: 1844-1895; editor of the 1875-1876 EETS source edition.

scope.description: Middle English rendering of the Guy of Warwick tradition
Redaction encompassing the Guy narrative and the integral Harrawde/Reinbrun continuation
Editorial presentation in EETS Extra Series 25-26

notes: Transcription, collation, normalization, and supplied punctuation belong to the edition layer and are also modeled in lineage.

Wiggins dates all Middle English redaction archetypes before the end of the fourteenth century. D-MER instead calls the Later Couplet version early fifteenth century; preserve that as a conflict rather than silently combining the dates.
The surviving Ff.2.38 copy is late fifteenth or early sixteenth century and must not be substituted for the redaction date.

places.label: South Lincolnshire
notes: Part of England; Wiggins's probable Kesteven-Holland-area linguistic localization.
Wiggins localizes the E-redaction archetype linguistically to the Lincoln/Kesteven-Holland area and concludes South Lincolnshire is probable.
This is a work/redaction-level linguistic inference. Do not transfer the surviving witness's production dialect or present Cambridge location to composition metadata.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The inherited U-M header code eng describes the modern delivery broadly; the medieval work text is Middle English.

form_statements.description: An 11,976-line Middle English romance in short rhyming couplets; prose edition matter and note quotations are excluded from the work boundary.

subject_statements.label: Heroic life, chivalric identity, exile, and return
Military combat and siege
notes: Treachery and stewardship are retained as separate normalized discovery tags, not folded into the canonical label for military-combat-and-siege.
D-MER groups several Guy versions on one work page; these discovery terms are retained at medium confidence rather than asserted as exhaustive for every movement in this redaction.

subject_statements.label: Penance, pilgrimage, religious spaces, and sacrament
Marriage, friendship, mistaken identity, recognition tokens, and succession through Reinbrun

summaries.value: The fullest continuous Middle English couplet redaction of Guy of Warwick traces Guy's chivalric career, exile, return, penance, and death, then continues integrally with the fortunes of his son Reinbrun and the associated Harrawde narrative. The surviving Ff.2.38 witness and Zupitza's edition present the continuation as part of one work despite a strong internal codicological and typographic boundary.

The record covers one 11,976-line redaction. Guy lines 1-10,786 and the Harrawde/Reinbrun continuation lines 10,787-11,976 are nested analytical movements with item_count null. Printed p.310 is shared across their boundary, whereas the manuscript ends the first movement on f.231ra, leaves f.231rb blank, and begins the continuation on f.231va.

extent.description: One verse work comprising 11,976 direct body poem lines in 40 direct body DIV1/LG groups. Sixteen L elements nested in editorial notes are excluded. The two nested analytical movements partition the direct poem lines and groups but have item_count null, so they do not inflate numbered_items=1.

content_parts.label: Guy of Warwick (Later Couplet version), complete redaction
content_parts.summary: The single continuous 11,976-line Middle English romance represented by the 40 direct body sections.

locators.scheme: XML nodes and direct poem lines
locators.value: DIV1 NODE ANZ4364.0001.001:2-41; direct poem lines 1-11976
161r-239v; final line on f.239va, first column

notes: This is the mutually exclusive counted representation of the one cataloged work.
DIMEV record 4908 describes 11,976 lines but gives a locus/last-lines boundary ending at the internal Guy closure; that conflict does not divide the cataloged work.

content_parts.label: Guy narrative
content_parts.summary: Analytical movement through Guy's conclusion and the explicit ending at lines 10,783-10,786; it is not a separately counted work.

locators.scheme: XML nodes and direct poem lines

notes: Printed p.310 is shared with the following movement; the line ranges remain non-overlapping.
The manuscript leaves f.231rb blank after this movement.

content_parts.label: Harrawde and Reinbrun continuation
content_parts.summary: Analytical movement beginning 'Lystenyth now' and carrying the integral narrative continuation through the final Amen; it is not a separately counted work.

locators.scheme: XML nodes and direct poem lines

locators.value: 231va-239v; final line on f.239va, first column

notes: Printed p.310 is shared with the preceding movement; XML node 38 begins after the p.310 PB element and therefore first lists p.311 even though its opening lines are physically on p.310.
Wiggins argues from layout and codicology that the continuation is integral to the same redaction rather than an independent work.

related_works.title: Gui de Warewic
notes: The relation is to the Anglo-Norman romance tradition at work level. No exact physical French exemplar is identified in this packet.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned source XML for anz4364 at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: HEADER; BODY DIV1 NODE ANZ4364.0001.001:2-41; direct BODY/DIV1/LG/L text
evidence.summary: Identifies the U-M item and Zupitza edition and preserves the complete 40-section, 11,976-line poem plus edition navigation and apparatus.

evidence.citation: ANZ4364 XML structural, page-boundary, manuscript-milestone, and raw-to-pinned normalization audit.
evidence.locator: Direct-line counts, excluded apparatus lines, nested movement boundary, printed-page overlap, milestone anomalies, and normalized equivalence
evidence.summary: Computes one verse work, 40 direct body sections/groups, 11,976 direct poem lines, 16 excluded note-nested lines, and exact bounded raw-to-pinned navigational enrichment.
notes: The raw package XML has SHA-256 4d1f15bdd01dc411a4c5c66b07dd89fc113f464f0fc5bca0a4001efc832d0caa. The pinned XML adds 42 DIV1/@NODE and 455 NOTE1/@ID values and changes EDITORIALDECL N from 1 to 4; after reversing exactly those changes and normalizing formatting whitespace, raw and pinned canonical forms match at SHA-256 a0e301b94b66fecea2489e60d2a6c16ad57308caca4591526bc0b69ddc77f21e.

evidence.citation: Julius Zupitza, ed., The Romance of Guy of Warwick: The Second or 15th-Century Version, EETS Extra Series 25-26 (1875-1876).
evidence.locator: Preface pp. vii-ix; text pp. 1-344; shared movement boundary on p.310; notes begin p.345
evidence.summary: Grounds the edition title, manuscript basis, line and page boundaries, Caius collation scope, editorial normalization, and one continuous 11,976-line text.

evidence.citation: Alison Wiggins, Guy of Warwick: Study and Transcription, PhD thesis, University of Sheffield, 2000.
evidence.summary: Provides redaction identity and date, Ff.2.38 codicology, exact ff.161ra-239vb locus, f.231 movement boundary, the continuation's integral status, and probable South Lincolnshire archetype origin.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, versioned record 4908 (NIMEV 3146).
evidence.summary: Supports the incipit, Later Couplet/Second Version identity, 11,976-line extent, couplet form, Ff.2.38 and Caius witnesses, and exact Caius collation ranges; its whole-record extent conflicts with its internal Guy-only terminal locator and last lines.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Guy of Warwick work page.
evidence.locator: Later Couplet version description and work-level keywords
evidence.summary: Supports the redaction label, romance classification, broad plot/subject discovery terms, and relationship to the Anglo-Norman tradition. The page groups several versions and therefore does not prove every keyword at every nested movement.
notes: Browser-readable during research; command-line retrieval returned HTTP 403, so no local cache is claimed.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38 page.
evidence.summary: Provides broad manuscript date, material, dimensions, scribal count, and a Guy ff.161r-231r locator, but omits the integral continuation; preserve that gap as a catalog conflict.
notes: Browser-readable during research; command-line retrieval returned HTTP 403, so no local cache is claimed.

evidence.citation: A Catalogue of the Manuscripts Preserved in the Library of the University of Cambridge, vol. 2 (Cambridge, 1857), Ff.II.38.
evidence.summary: Describes the whole codex and item 40, including the history of Heraud through the old foliation endpoint. Modern Wiggins/Dixon analysis supersedes or qualifies its approximate physical description and locators.

open_questions.question: Should future scholarship prefer a fourteenth-century or early-fifteenth-century date for the Later Couplet redaction?
next_steps: Retain Wiggins's detailed pre-1400 conclusion at medium confidence.
Preserve D-MER's early-fifteenth-century statement as an attributed conflict and review future scholarship.

open_questions.question: Which Anglo-Norman textual state or physical exemplar underlies this Middle English redaction?
next_steps: Keep a work/tradition-level adaptation relation rather than asserting a manuscript-level French source path.
Review future stemmatic or source studies for an exact exemplar claim.

open_questions.question: Where did the original EETS Extra Series 25 volume end and number 26 begin?
next_steps: Record two serial parts without assigning an unverified exact page boundary.
Inspect original wrappers or a bibliographic collation if the serial boundary becomes material.

notes: Treat the record as one work with two nested analytical movements; do not split the continuation into a second counted item.
The 40 direct body DIV1/LG groups are encoding and edition-navigation units, not 40 independent works and not a proved stanza count.

The source edition was issued as EETS Extra Series numbers 25 and 26 in 1875-1876. The exact original serial-part page boundary has not been verified and must not be invented from the later bound scan.
The source edition's title calls this the second or fifteenth-century version, but the surviving witness date and the redaction's proposed composition date are separate statements.
